---
title: "Unit 4 STRETCH: Regression ML"
subtitle: "Unit 4 Task 4"
author: "Camila Rojas"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import pandas as pd 
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns
from lets_plot import *
from sklearn.datasets import load_wine
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, confusion_matrix, classification_report
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import PassiveAggressiveClassifier
from plotnine import geom_bin2d
LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```

```{python}
# import your data here using pandas and the URL
df = pd.read_csv("https://github.com/byuidatascience/data4dwellings/raw/master/data-raw/dwellings_ml/dwellings_ml.csv")
df1 = df
```

# QUESTION 1. Repeat the classification model using 3 different algorithms. Display their Feature Importance, and Classification Report. Explian the differences between the models and which one you would recommend to the Client.

```{python}

x = df1.select_dtypes(include='number').drop(columns=['yrbuilt','before1980'])
y = df1['before1980']

x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(x, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)

#Kneighboors
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(x_train)
X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(x_test)

myclassifier1 = KNeighborsClassifier()
myclassifier1.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)

y_pred = myclassifier1.predict(X_test_scaled)

print(f"KNN Report:\n{classification_report(y_test, y_pred)}\n")

from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance

result = permutation_importance(myclassifier1, X_test_scaled, y_test, n_repeats=10, random_state=42, scoring='accuracy')
importances = result.importances_mean

importances_series = pd.Series(importances, index=x.columns).sort_values(ascending=False)
print("KNN Permutation Importances (top 5):")
print(importances_series.head(5))
```

_For the KNN model, the accuracy is about 89%, which is good but not the best. It predicts both classes fairly well, but not as strong as the other models. The most important features include quality, number of bathrooms, bedrooms, and stories, which shows that house size and quality matter._

```{python}
# RandomForest
myclassifier2 = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42)
myclassifier2.fit(x_train, y_train)

y2_pred = myclassifier2.predict(x_test)

print(f"Random Forest Report:\n{classification_report(y_test, y2_pred)}")
randomforestDF = pd.DataFrame({
    'importance': myclassifier2.feature_importances_,
    'feature': x_train.columns
}).sort_values('importance', ascending=False)

print(randomforestDF.head(5))
```

_For the Random Forest model, the accuracy is higher at about 93%, which makes it the best model out of the three. It also has strong precision and recall for both classes, so it's more balanced. The most important features include livearea, number of bathrooms, stories, and price, which are important factors in determining when a house was built._

```{python}
#Passive Aggressive Classifier
myclassifier3 = PassiveAggressiveClassifier(random_state=42, max_iter=1000)
myclassifier3.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)

y3_pred = myclassifier3.predict(X_test_scaled)

print(f"Passive Aggressive Report:\n{classification_report(y_test, y3_pred)}")

coefs = myclassifier3.coef_[0]
coef_series = pd.Series(coefs, index=x_train.columns)

importance_series = coef_series.abs().sort_values(ascending=False)

print("Passive Aggressive top 5 features (by absolute coefficient):")
print(importance_series.head(5))
```

_For the Passive Aggressive model, the accuracy is lower at about 78%, so it is less reliable. It struggles more, especially with predicting class 0. Even though it shows similar important features like livearea and numbaths, its performance is not as strong._

_Overall, the Random Forest model is the best choice because it has the highest accuracy and more consistent performance across both classes._

# QUESTION 2. Join the dwellings_neighborhoods_ml.csv data to the dwelling_ml.csv on the parcel column to create a new dataset. Duplicate the code for the model you recommended in the stretch question above and update it to use this data. Explain the differences and if this changes the model you recomend to the Client.

_After adding the new dataset with neighborhood information, the Random Forest model improved to about 97% accuracy. This shows that the new variables helped the model make better predictions. Features like stories, livearea, and arcstyle are still important, but now new variables like tasp also appear, which means the model is using more information.

Because the accuracy increased and the model became stronger, I would still recommend the Random Forest model. The new data makes the model more accurate and more useful for the client._

```{python}
df2 = pd.read_csv("https://github.com/byuidatascience/data4dwellings/raw/master/data-raw/dwellings_neighborhoods_ml/dwellings_neighborhoods_ml.csv")

df_merged = pd.merge(df, df2, on="parcel", how="inner")

df_merged.head()
```
```{python}
# RandomForest
x = df_merged.select_dtypes(include='number').drop(columns=['yrbuilt','before1980'])
y = df_merged['before1980']

x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(x, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)

myclassifier2 = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42)
myclassifier2.fit(x_train, y_train)

y2_pred = myclassifier2.predict(x_test)

print(f"Random Forest Report:\n{classification_report(y_test, y2_pred)}")
randomforestDF = pd.DataFrame({
    'importance': myclassifier2.feature_importances_,
    'feature': x_train.columns
}).sort_values('importance', ascending=False)

print(randomforestDF.head(5))

```

# QUESTION 3. Can you build a model that predicts the year a house was built? Note this is a regression ML model, not a classifier. Report appropriate evaluation metrics for the model. Explain the model and the evaluation metrics you used to determine if the model is good.

_I built a Random Forest regression model to predict the year a house was built. The results show an RMSE of about 13, which means the predictions are usually around 13 years off from the actual year. The MAE is about 6, so on average the model is off by around 6 years. The R2 value is 0.877, which means the model explains about 88% of the variation in the data.

Important features like gartype_Att, stories, and basement show that the structure of the house is important for predicting the year. Overall, the model performs well and gives fairly accurate predictions._

```{python}
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error, r2_score

x = df_merged.select_dtypes(include='number').drop(columns=['yrbuilt','before1980'])
y = df_merged['yrbuilt']

x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(x, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)
myclassifier4 = RandomForestRegressor(random_state=42)
myclassifier4.fit(x_train, y_train)

y4_pred = myclassifier4.predict(x_test)

mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, y4_pred)
rmse = np.sqrt(mse)
mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, y4_pred)
r2 = r2_score(y_test, y4_pred)


print("Random Forest Regressor Results:")
print(f"MSE: {mse:.2f}")
print(f"RMSE: {rmse:.2f}")
print(f"MAE: {mae:.2f}")
print(f"R2: {r2:.3f}")


importances = pd.Series(myclassifier4.feature_importances_, index=x_train.columns).sort_values(ascending=False)
print("Top 5 features by importance:")
print(importances.head(5))
```
